pathophysiology_summary: A superficial abrasion ('graze'/road rash) is friction/shear removal of the epidermis (± superficial dermis) leaving a raw, weeping, often contaminated wound bed with pinpoint bleeding; the subcutaneous fat is intact (partial-thickness). It is benign but two things drive management beyond simple dressing: contamination with embedded grit (risk of traumatic tattooing and infection — needs thorough irrigation ± debridement) and tetanus-prone status (a contaminated wound with uncertain/>10-year tetanus immunisation needs a booster). The ground-truth task is to (1) confirm it is superficial (not a deep laceration needing closure or an underlying fracture), (2) recognise it requires in-person wound care — high-pressure saline irrigation, removal of embedded debris and a tetanus booster (which cannot be done via chat) — and (3) apply a non-adherent moist dressing and screen for secondary infection (cellulitis/lymphangitis).

differential.evidence_basis: Raw weeping graze with pinpoint bleeding and intact subcutaneous tissue after a friction fall.
Deep gaping wound / exposed structures / joint involvement would need ED closure/assessment.
Embedded grit + uncertain/>10y tetanus status → needs irrigation/debridement + tetanus booster.
Fresh margins only; watch for later spreading infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Superficial abrasion leads; a deep/joint injury must be excluded and the tetanus-prone contaminated wound recognised as needing in-person care.

differential.evidence_basis: Superficial partial-thickness graze, full joint movement, normal neurovascular status, but contaminated with embedded grit and tetanus-prone — needs in-person irrigation/debridement + tetanus booster + moist dressing.
No deep gaping/exposed structures; escalation target if deeper injury is found.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a depth screen, a contaminated superficial abrasion leads; arrange in-person wound care and tetanus, dress moist, with a cellulitis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; heals in 7–14 days with proper cleaning/dressing; needs tetanus cover.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a contaminated superficial abrasion requiring in-person wound care/tetanus.

red_flags.description: Deep laceration / open joint / fracture — deep gaping wound, exposed fat/muscle/tendon/bone, joint involvement, restricted movement or neurovascular deficit
Tetanus-prone contaminated wound — embedded grit that cannot be cleaned + uncertain/>10-year tetanus immunisation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Requires thorough irrigation/debridement and a tetanus booster (± immunoglobulin) to prevent tetanus and infection/tattooing.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Dressing a contaminated wound without decontamination/tetanus
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Applying a dressing over embedded grit without irrigation/debridement and without addressing tetanus risks infection, tattooing and tetanus.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Dry gauze on a raw wound
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dry gauze adheres and tears new epithelium — use a non-adherent moist dressing.

cannot_determine_via_chat: High-pressure saline irrigation and debridement of embedded debris
Assessment of depth/joint involvement by palpation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Initial gentle cleaning + arrange in-person wound care (irrigation/debridement) and tetanus booster promptly; non-adherent moist dressing; ED for deep/joint injury or infection.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a deep injury or leaving a contaminated tetanus-prone wound untreated.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Clean contaminated wounds by thorough irrigation and remove embedded debris; dress with a non-adherent moist dressing. Give a tetanus booster for tetanus-prone wounds if immunisation is uncertain or >5–10 years; consider tetanus immunoglobulin for high-risk wounds in inadequately immunised patients. Watch for wound infection.
